edges.description: Constitutive MAPK activation in neural progenitors during development drives aberrant proliferation and dual differentiation toward both neuronal and glial lineages, producing the characteristic mixed cellular composition.
The mixed neuronal-glial proliferation forms a circumscribed, slow-growing (CNS WHO grade 1) tumor, typically in the cerebral cortex with a predilection for the temporal and frontal lobes.
The cortical tumor, frequently associated with adjacent focal cortical dysplasia, disrupts local cortical circuitry and creates an epileptogenic zone with high intrinsic epileptogenic potential.
The epileptogenic cortical substrate manifests clinically as chronic, frequently drug-resistant focal seizures, the dominant presenting phenotype of these tumors.

evidence.snippet: The most common mutation was BRAFV600E present in 36.94%, followed by the mutation in FGFR1, reported in 1.18% of cases.

nodes.description: For BRAF p.V600E-mutant tumors that are progressive, recurrent, or unresectable, combined BRAF and MEK inhibition (e.g., dabrafenib plus trametinib) is the preferred targeted approach; BRAF V600E is the only molecular target of proven clinical benefit in adult gliomas/glioneuronal tumors per EANO. In a multi-institutional series of BRAF-mutant pediatric high-grade gliomas (including anaplastic gangliogliomas) treated upfront, 3-year progression-free and overall survival were 65% and 82%.

evidence.snippet: From all targets reviewed, only testing for BRAF V600E mutations was of proven clinical benefit

three-year progression-free and overall survival for the cohort were 65% and 82%, respectively, and superior to a historical control cohort of BRAF-mutant pHGG patients treated with conventional therapies.

nodes.description: A circumscribed, indolent (most commonly CNS WHO grade 1) neoplasm situated in the cerebral cortex, most often the temporal lobe. The cortical/subcortical location and intimate relationship with surrounding cortex underlie both the typical seizure presentation and the favorable outcome after gross total resection.

evidence.snippet: Rare glial, neuronal and glioneuronal tumours in adults form a heterogeneous group of rare, primary central nervous system tumours.

nodes.description: Chronic focal-onset seizures, frequently medically refractory, are the dominant clinical presentation of mixed neuronal-glial tumors, especially ganglioglioma and DNET. Onset is typically in childhood to young adulthood with a long pre-diagnosis history.

evidence.snippet: Structural brain lesions, including the broad range of malformations of cortical development (MCD) and glioneuronal tumors, are among the most common causes of drug-resistant focal epilepsy.
Most of these tumors occur in young patients and are related to epilepsy.

nodes.description: Aberrant differentiation of MAPK-activated neural progenitors yields a neoplasm containing both a neoplastic neuronal component (dysmorphic ganglion cells, floating neurons, or neurocytic rosettes depending on subtype) and a neoplastic glial component (astrocytic GFAP-positive and/or oligodendrocyte-like OLIG2-positive cells). This dual lineage is the defining histological feature of glioneuronal tumors and distinguishes them from pure gliomas.

evidence.snippet: Glioneuronal tumors are characterized exclusively by neurocytic elements (neuronal tumors) or a combination of neuronal and glial features (mixed neuronal-glial tumors).

nodes.description: Glioneuronal tumors, particularly ganglioglioma and DNET, are among the most common causes of drug-resistant focal epilepsy. The intimate cortical location, abnormal neuronal differentiation, and frequent association with focal cortical dysplasia generate a highly epileptogenic substrate, such that these tumors have been informally termed "epileptomas".

evidence.snippet: Structural brain lesions, including the broad range of malformations of cortical development (MCD) and glioneuronal tumors, are among the most common causes of drug-resistant focal epilepsy.

nodes.description: The initiating and central pathogenic event is a somatic genetic alteration that constitutively activates the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK (MAPK) signaling cascade. In ganglioglioma the dominant driver is the BRAF p.V600E missense mutation (glutamic acid-to-valine substitution in the kinase domain), while in DNET and other entities FGFR1 hotspot mutations (N546K, K656E) predominate; FGFR1 activation simultaneously stimulates the RAS/RAF/MEK and PI3K-AKT cascades. These low-grade glioneuronal tumors are considered developmental neoplasms arising from MAPK-activated neural progenitors rather than from environmental exposures.

evidence.snippet: Low-grade gliomas (LGG) and mixed neuronal-glial tumors (MNGT) show frequent MAPK pathway alterations.
The most common mutation was BRAFV600E present in 36.94%, followed by the mutation in FGFR1, reported in 1.18% of cases.

nodes.description: Gross total surgical resection is the cornerstone of treatment and is considered curative for most CNS WHO grade 1 glioneuronal tumors. For epilepsy-associated tumors, extended resection that includes the epileptogenic zone improves seizure-free outcomes.
